questions.question: Define the following: (i) Simple graph (ii) Complete graph (iii) Regular graph
keyPoints: Simple graph: no self-loops and no multiple edges
Complete graph Kn: every pair of vertices is connected by an edge
Regular graph: all vertices have the same degree (k-regular means degree k for all vertices)

questions.question: Explain the difference between adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations of a graph. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Adjacency matrix: O(V²) space, O(1) edge lookup, good for dense graphs
Adjacency list: O(V+E) space, O(degree) lookup, good for sparse graphs
Prefer matrix when graph is dense (E close to V²)
Prefer list when graph is sparse (E << V²)

questions.question: State and prove the Handshaking Lemma for undirected graphs.
keyPoints: Handshaking Lemma: Sum of degrees = 2×E
Proof: Each edge contributes to degree of 2 vertices
Therefore counting edge contributions gives total degree sum which equals 2E
Used to prove many graph properties

questions.question: What is a weighted graph? Give an example of its application in real-world scenarios.
keyPoints: Weighted graph: assigns numerical weight to each edge
Weights represent cost, distance, time, capacity, etc.
Examples: road networks (distance/time), airline routes (fare), network routing (latency), supply chain (cost)

questions.question: Define: (i) Isomorphic graphs (ii) Homeomorphic graphs
keyPoints: Isomorphic: one-to-one vertex mapping preserving adjacency
Homeomorphic: can be obtained from same graph by subdividing edges
Isomorphism preserves exact structure; homeomorphism preserves topological properties

questions.question: What is the minimum number of edges in a connected graph with n vertices? Explain.
keyPoints: Minimum edges = n-1
This forms a spanning tree
A connected graph with n-1 edges must be a tree (no cycles)
Adding any edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle

questions.question: Differentiate between directed and undirected graphs with respect to degree calculation.
keyPoints: Undirected: single degree (number of incident edges)
Directed: indegree (edges coming in) and outdegree (edges going out)
In directed: sum of indegrees = sum of outdegrees = number of edges

questions.question: Explain Dijkstra's algorithm for finding shortest paths in a weighted graph. Illustrate with an example having at least 5 vertices.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Start from source with distance 0, others ∞
Use priority queue to repeatedly extract minimum distance vertex
Relax edges: if new path shorter, update distance
Continue until all vertices processed or queue empty
Works only with non-negative weights
Time: O((V+E) log V) with priority queue, Space: O(V)
Example should show step-by-step updates

questions.question: Discuss the Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms for finding Minimum Spanning Tree (MST). Compare their time complexities and applications.
keyPoints: Kruskal's: Sort all edges, add smallest that doesn't form cycle (Union-Find)
Prim's: Start from vertex, grow tree by adding minimum weight edge connecting to unvisited vertex
Kruskal's: O(E log E), better for sparse graphs
Prim's: O(V²) or O(E log V) with heap, better for dense graphs
Both are greedy algorithms producing MST

questions.question: Define bipartite graph. How can you test whether a graph is bipartite? Explain with an algorithm.
keyPoints: Bipartite: vertices can be partitioned into two sets where edges only cross between sets
Test using BFS/DFS coloring: assign two colors to alternating vertices
If conflict found (adjacent vertices same color), graph is not bipartite
Odd cycle detection: graph is bipartite iff it contains no odd cycles
Algorithm: BFS/DFS with color array, O(V+E)

questions.question: Explain the Bellman-Ford algorithm for shortest paths. How does it handle negative edge weights? What is its time and space complexity?
keyPoints: Initialize distances: source = 0, others = ∞
Relax all edges V-1 times: if d[u] + w < d[v], update d[v]
Can detect negative weight cycles: if any edge can be relaxed in Vth iteration, negative cycle exists
Handles negative weights unlike Dijkstra
Time: O(VE), Space: O(V)
